best_practices.overview: Non-negotiable quality standards for the generated multi-agent system backend. These rules apply to every file generated by multi-agent-system-builder mode. The stack is: FastAPI + LangGraph + ChatOllama (langchain-ollama).

security.rule: NEVER hardcode credentials, API keys, tokens, or secrets in any generated file. All secrets must come from environment variables loaded via pydantic-settings.
The .env.example file must use placeholder values only (YOUR_API_KEY, not a real key). The real .env file must be listed in .gitignore.

All user-provided input entering the system must be validated by a Pydantic model at the API boundary before being passed to the graph. Never pass raw request body strings directly to graph.ainvoke().
Error responses to clients must never include internal stack traces, file paths, or agent system prompts. Use generic error messages for 5xx responses.
Authentication (if required) must be implemented as a FastAPI dependency, applied consistently to all protected routes — not per-route ad-hoc checks.
security.gitignore_minimum: # Always include in .gitignore: .env __pycache__/ *.pyc *.pyo .venv/ venv/ *.egg-info/ dist/ build/ .DS_Store

naming_conventions.rule: All Python identifiers: snake_case. No exceptions.
Node names (passed to add_node()) follow: domain_role or verb_noun (e.g., research_agent, summarise_node)
Tool functions: snake_case verb_object (e.g., fetch_web_content, validate_schema)
State classes: PascalCase TypedDict (e.g., SupervisorState, RoutedState)
Pydantic API models: PascalCase (e.g., ChatRequest, ChatResponse)
File names: snake_case.py — no spaces, no hyphens

checklist.item: Node name strings in add_node() match the function/subgraph being added
WORKER_AGENTS list and conditional_edges routing map are in sync
thread_id passed as config={"configurable": {"thread_id": session_id}} on every ainvoke()
Environment variable names: UPPER_SNAKE_CASE matching pydantic field names

error_handling.rule: Node functions must catch exceptions internally and return an AIMessage with the error description. Never let an unhandled exception propagate out of a graph node.

FastAPI route handlers must catch graph invocation errors and map to HTTP status codes: - LangGraph/LLM error → 500 Internal Server Error - Ollama unreachable → 503 Service Unavailable - Pydantic ValidationError → 422 Unprocessable Entity (automatic) - Missing thread_id → 400 Bad Request (validate in route)
Use try/except with specific exception types. Avoid bare except: clauses.

logging.rule: Use Python's standard logging module — never print() in production code.
All log entries should include the session_id or node name for traceability.

Log level guidelines: - DEBUG: detailed state (messages content, routing decisions) - INFO: normal events (graph compiled, request received, node completed) - WARNING: recoverable issues (Ollama warm-up failed, model slow to respond) - ERROR: failures that impact the user (graph crashed, Ollama unreachable)
Never log full system prompts or full message history at INFO or above — leaks sensitive instructions.

performance.rule: All I/O operations (Ollama calls, HTTP requests) must be async. No blocking calls inside async functions.
ChatOllama instances are lightweight — creating one per request is acceptable, but reusing via get_llm() is cleaner.
For independent parallel agent calls, use asyncio.gather() inside a single node or LangGraph's Send API.
Set explicit timeouts on all ChatOllama calls via the timeout parameter. Default: 120s minimum. Never 30s.
Local LLM inference is slow — 120s is the minimum safe timeout on modest hardware.
Add model warm-up in lifespan to prevent 30–90s cold-start on the first user request (see 4_provider_ollama.xml).
performance.common_mistake:
# BAD — sync graph.invoke() called inside async FastAPI route (blocks the event loop) @router.post("/chat") async def chat(request: ChatRequest, req: Request): result = req.app.state.graph.invoke(...) # WRONG: use ainvoke
# GOOD — always await the async version @router.post("/chat") async def chat(request: ChatRequest, req: Request): result = await req.app.state.graph.ainvoke(...) # CORRECT

langgraph_specifics.rule: Always pass thread_id in config when calling graph.ainvoke() — without it, MemorySaver cannot scope state to a session, and all users share the same conversation history.
WORKER_AGENTS list and conditional_edges routing map must be kept in sync. Adding a worker node without adding it to the routing map causes the supervisor to silently fall through to END.
create_react_agent() returns a compiled graph — add it with builder.add_node(), not as a function. It handles its own internal loop.
MemorySaver is not thread-safe for concurrent writes to the same thread_id. For multi-process deployments, use SqliteSaver or a Redis-backed checkpointer.
State reducers (Annotated[list, operator.add]) are required for parallel branches that write to the same field — without them, concurrent writes overwrite each other.

testing.rule: Every graph node function must have at least one unit test calling it directly with a mock state dict.
The compiled graph should have at least one integration test using a mock LLM.
Tests live in backend/tests/ — use pytest-asyncio with asyncio_mode = "auto" (see pyproject_template in 8_code_generation.xml).
testing.mock_llm_guidance:
Use Phase 0 Step 4 confirmed results for message class names and import paths. Verify: message class names and import paths from Phase 0 output before writing tests. The pytest/AsyncMock patterns are stable; the LangChain message classes must be MCP-confirmed.
Structural pattern: from unittest.mock import AsyncMock, patch [import message classes confirmed via MCP] from app.graph.nodes import your_node
async def test_your_node_happy_path(): state = {"messages": [[HumanMessage](content="test input")]} [confirm class via MCP] with patch("app.graph.nodes.get_llm") as mock_get_llm: mock_llm = AsyncMock() mock_llm.ainvoke.return_value = [AIMessage](content="expected") [confirm via MCP] mock_get_llm.return_value = mock_llm result = await your_node(state) assert "messages" in result assert "expected" in result["messages"][0].content
async def test_your_node_error_path(): state = {"messages": [[HumanMessage](content="test input")]} with patch("app.graph.nodes.get_llm") as mock_get_llm: mock_llm = AsyncMock() mock_llm.ainvoke.side_effect = Exception("Ollama connection refused") mock_get_llm.return_value = mock_llm result = await your_node(state) assert "messages" in result assert "Error" in result["messages"][0].content
Key principles (stable regardless of API version): - Patch get_llm, not ChatOllama directly — isolates node logic from the LLM - Always test BOTH happy path and error path per node - Pass state as a plain dict — no need for actual LangGraph state classes in unit tests

production_hardening.overview: Production gaps that are invisible during local development but cause failures or security issues in deployed multi-agent systems. Address these before shipping.

item.title: Persistence: replace MemorySaver
item.problem: MemorySaver stores conversation state in process memory — all history is lost on restart, and it is NOT safe for concurrent writes to the same thread_id across multiple worker processes.
item.solution: For production use SqliteSaver (single-process) or PostgresSaver (multi-process/multi-worker). MANDATORY: Query MCP before implementing any saver other than MemorySaver: search_docs_by_lang_chain("LangGraph SqliteSaver PostgresSaver checkpointer production current") The saver constructors and connection API change across LangGraph versions — MCP is authoritative. In lifespan, create and close the saver connection (it may hold file locks or DB connections).

item.title: Authentication: FastAPI dependency
item.problem: API key validation scattered across routes or handled ad-hoc is easy to miss on new routes.
item.solution: Implement a single FastAPI dependency: async def verify_api_key(x_api_key: str = Header(...)) -> None: if settings.api_key and x_api_key != settings.api_key: raise HTTPException(status_code=401, detail="Invalid API key") Apply with `dependencies=[Depends(verify_api_key)]` on the APIRouter, not per-route. When api_key setting is empty, the dependency passes through (no auth for local dev). This is standard FastAPI — no MCP query required.

item.title: Rate limiting: slowapi
item.problem: Local LLM inference is slow and resource-intensive. Without rate limiting, a single abusive client can block all other users or exhaust system memory.
item.solution: Use `slowapi` (FastAPI-native rate limiter): pip install slowapi from slowapi import Limiter from slowapi.util import get_remote_address limiter = Limiter(key_func=get_remote_address) @router.post("/chat") @limiter.limit("10/minute") async def chat(request: Request, body: ChatRequest): ... Add limiter to app state and register the SlowAPIMiddleware — see slowapi docs for current setup. Adjust limit per your hardware's inference throughput.

item.title: Observability: structured logging + LangSmith
item.problem: Log lines without structure are hard to filter. LLM call failures without traces are impossible to debug retroactively.
item.solution: Structured logging: add `python-json-logger` and emit logs as JSON in production: if settings.environment == "production": handler.setFormatter(pythonjsonlogger.jsonlogger.JsonFormatter(...)) LangSmith tracing (optional but powerful): set env vars LANGCHAIN_TRACING_V2=true, LANGCHAIN_API_KEY, LANGCHAIN_PROJECT — LangGraph emits traces automatically with no code changes. Add to .env.example as commented-out optional fields. Never log full message history or system prompts at INFO or above.

item.title: Concurrency: MemorySaver thread-safety
item.problem: MemorySaver uses an in-process dict with no locking. Under uvicorn --workers > 1, each worker has its own MemorySaver and different requests from the same session_id will land in different workers, fragmenting conversation history.
item.solution: Development: uvicorn with 1 worker (default) — MemorySaver is safe. Production: switch to SqliteSaver (same machine, 1 process) or PostgresSaver (any topology). Document this constraint in the README. Never run uvicorn --workers > 1 with MemorySaver.

item.title: Graceful shutdown: in-flight request handling
item.problem: SIGTERM during an LLM inference call (30–120s) aborts the request mid-response. Users get a 500 error and may retry, creating duplicate work.
item.solution: FastAPI lifespan handles this correctly when used with uvicorn --graceful-timeout. In the lifespan yield → shutdown block, log a shutdown message. Set `--graceful-timeout 130` (> max LLM timeout) in the start script so uvicorn waits for in-flight requests to complete before killing workers. Add to scripts/start.sh: `uvicorn app.main:app --graceful-timeout 130`

item.title: Extended health check: deep status endpoint
item.problem: A simple 200 OK health check doesn't reveal that Ollama is down or the graph failed to compile — the system looks healthy to load balancers while serving 500s.
item.solution: Extend GET /health to return component status: { "status": "ok" | "degraded" | "error", "ollama": "connected" | "unreachable", "graph": "compiled" | "not_compiled", "default_model": settings.default_model, "environment": settings.environment } "status": "degraded" when ollama is unreachable (agents will fail). "status": "error" when graph is not compiled (all requests will fail). Return HTTP 503 when status is "error" so load balancers route away from the instance.

category.name: before_generating
category.item: implementation.md has been read and all required sections are present
Node names from implementation.md are snake_case
Provider strategy confirmed as Ollama only (ChatOllama via langchain-ollama)

category.name: after_generating_each_file
category.item: All async functions only call other async functions or use await
All node functions catch exceptions and return AIMessage errors
All imports resolve to files that exist or will be generated in this session

category.name: before_completion
category.item: thread_id is passed in every graph.ainvoke() call
WORKER_AGENTS list matches all nodes added to the graph
setup.sh pulls all Ollama models listed in implementation.md
.env.example has all variables from config.py Settings class
README has clear setup and run instructions
Manual steps checklist has been presented to the user
